items.title: very notable trajectory comparison writeup here buried in the RLM paper from @a1…
items.summaryText:
very notable trajectory comparison writeup here buried in the RLM paper from @a1zhang and @lateinteraction.

an open secret of "frontier" model training is that even without training on test, you can basically cheat by training on test lookalikes, enabling you to goalseek almost any benchmark number you want.

however when they are released open weights, 99% of the time the norm is that you do not get the datasets/rlenvs that would easily show you if someone was training on Temu Tbench, so there is plausible deniability. Alex and Omar discuss applying standard NLP distance metrics on hidden trajectories. There's no ultimate solution here, but they have some prelim explorations. It happens to support the finding that RLMs can generalize to unseen tasks that share latent structure observed in training.

items.summaryZh:
这是一篇非常值得注意的轨迹比较文章，来自@a1zhang和@lateinteraction，隐藏在RLM论文中。

“前沿”模型训练的一个公开秘密是，即使没有在测试集上训练，你也可以通过训练与测试集相似的数据来作弊，从而实现几乎任何基准分数的目标导向。

然而，当它们以开放权重发布时，99%的情况下，你不会得到那些能轻易显示是否有人在Temu Tbench上训练的数据集/强化学习环境，因此存在合理的可否认性。Alex和Omar讨论了在隐藏轨迹上应用标准NLP距离度量。这里没有最终的解决方案，但他们进行了一些初步探索。这恰好支持了RLMs可以泛化到与训练中观察到的潜在结构共享的未见任务这一发现。

items.title:
If I were to hire today, this is how I would structure the interview process:

R…
items.summaryText:
If I were to hire today, this is how I would structure the interview process:

Round 1: in-person & no AI allowed; test domain expertise and knowledge on the fly

Round 2: a project that must be done with AI (impossible to complete without AI). Candidate assessed not just on the result but also the chat transcript with agents

如果今天我招聘，我会这样安排面试流程：

第一轮：现场面试，不允许使用AI；测试领域专业知识和即时反应能力

第二轮：一个必须使用AI完成的项目（没有AI无法完成）。不仅评估结果，还评估与AI助手的聊天记录

items.title: Multi-model agentic systems clearly are the future. Great post by cursor. Their…
items.summaryText:
Multi-model agentic systems clearly are the future. Great post by cursor. Their new research shows that a frontier model as a planner and orchestrator with a workhorse cheaper model can materially lower the cost of total tokens on a project, resulting in a 15X cost improvement.

“Few moments in a large task genuinely require frontier intelligence, such as the original decomposition, the design decisions, and certain trade-offs. Once a frontier planner has collapsed the ambiguity into a detailed, explicit instruction, less expensive models simply have to follow it.”

This is increasingly becoming the core design pattern of complex agents, because the tokens used on large amounts of work on many tasks don’t require the same intelligence threshold as the planning step. By routing to different models based on where you are in the task, you get much greater efficiency overall. 

This provides the template for where the applied layer in AI will differentiate. You can only drive this efficiency if you know the domain well and have the ability to work with multiple model tiers; companies that can do this will across coding, finance, legal, healthcare, life sciences, and other critical spaces will be in a strong position to get larger workloads that would otherwise be too expensive for the customer to deploy.

metadata.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

items.summaryZh:
多模型代理系统显然是未来。cursor发布了一篇很棒的文章。他们的新研究表明，一个前沿模型作为规划者和协调者，配合一个更便宜的工作模型，可以大幅降低项目中总token的成本，实现15倍的成本改善。

“在一个大型任务中，真正需要前沿智能的时刻很少，比如初始分解、设计决策和某些权衡。一旦前沿规划者将模糊性转化为详细明确的指令，较便宜的模型只需遵循即可。”

这正成为复杂代理的核心设计模式，因为许多任务中大量工作所使用的token并不需要与规划步骤相同的智能门槛。通过根据任务所处阶段路由到不同的模型，你可以获得更高的整体效率。

这为AI应用层的差异化提供了模板。只有深入了解领域并具备使用多模型层级的能力，才能推动这种效率；能够做到这一点的公司将在编码、金融、法律、医疗、生命科学和其他关键领域获得更大的工作负载，而这些负载原本对客户来说成本过高而无法部署。

items.title: Many founders in the last 18 months are going to realize that just because there…
items.summaryText:
Many founders in the last 18 months are going to realize that just because there are no “moats” in AI left anymore doesn’t mean “scale and capital” become your main moat.

We have seen this movie before.. all of us will benefit from reading a little bit of history.

Webvan
Groupon
MySpace
Yahoo
AltaVista
Blockbuster
Nokia
(zombiecorns of 2021)

Each one was structurally and capital-wise in a very good position, but eventually got beat by a company with a much better unique insight. Or just collapsed under the weight of their own scale. 

This is the needle founders have to thread: find a unique insight worth a 10+ year journey, while being prudent enough to not let capital & scale become a substitute for it.

metadata.bio: partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧

items.summaryZh:
过去18个月中的许多创始人将意识到，仅仅因为AI领域不再有“护城河”，并不意味着“规模和资本”就成了你的主要护城河。

我们以前见过这种情况……大家都该读点历史，会受益的。

Webvan
Groupon
MySpace
Yahoo
AltaVista
Blockbuster
Nokia
(2021年的独角尸)

每一个在结构和资本上都处于非常有利的位置，但最终被一家拥有更好独特洞察的公司击败。或者仅仅在自身规模的重压下崩溃。

这就是创始人必须穿针引线的地方：找到一个值得十年以上旅程的独特洞察，同时足够谨慎，不让资本和规模成为它的替代品。

items.title:
Use one agent to do the work and another to review it against a rubric.

@trq212…
items.summaryText:
Use one agent to do the work and another to review it against a rubric.

@trq212 explains how this works for video shorts:

“For something like, ‘Is this a good video short or not?’ you don’t have a deterministic answer.

You should have a separate verification agent read a rubric, review the short, and give feedback.

We call it self-preferential bias. When a model prefers its own output, it’s going to be more lenient at verifying it.”

📌 Watch the full episode here: https://t.co/mMrt4D5qU7

metadata.bio: Practical AI tutorials and interviews for busy people | Get my best AI skills and guides at https://t.co/6VAA6p81x6

items.summaryZh:
用一个智能体来执行工作，另一个根据评分标准进行审核。

@trq212 解释了这在视频短片中的工作原理：

“对于像‘这是一个好视频短片吗？’这样的问题，你没有一个确定的答案。

你应该有一个独立的验证智能体读取评分标准，审查短片并给出反馈。

我们称之为自我偏好偏差。当模型偏好自己的输出时，它在验证时会更加宽容。”

📌 在此观看完整集：https://t.co/mMrt4D5qU7

items.title: There are basically two kinds of companies now. The ones built before coding age…
items.summaryText:
There are basically two kinds of companies now. The ones built before coding agents showed up, scrambling to retrofit. And the ones founded after. 

The second type is different from day one:
- Teams under ten people because they actually don't need more
- Work organized by projects, not departments
- Each person closes their own loop
- Almost no internal meetings

metadata.bio: Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: https://t.co/KCuEajezlL YouTube: https://t.co/8xzbGWtf6w

items.summaryZh:
现在基本上有两种公司。一种是在编码智能体出现之前建立的，还在匆忙改造。另一种是之后创立的。

第二种从第一天起就不同：
- 团队不到十人，因为他们实际上不需要更多
- 工作按项目组织，而不是按部门
- 每个人自己闭环
- 几乎没有内部会议

items.title: Travel Through the Lens of AI with with Booking.com CEO Glenn Fogel
items.summaryText:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:13
There is no such thing as a moat. There is no such thing as somewhere you're gonna be protected against innovation. Today, we have a competitive advantage on areas. Absolutely. But those can go away tomorrow.

Speaker 1 | 00:13 - 00:24
The only one to wi…
items.bodyText:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:13
There is no such thing as a moat. There is no such thing as somewhere you're gonna be protected against innovation. Today, we have a competitive advantage on areas. Absolutely. But those can go away tomorrow.

Speaker 1 | 00:13 - 00:24
The only one to win long term is to continue to develop new services, new ways to do things. How can we do your business better? What do you need? Where you need more demand? Where are you hurting?

Speaker 1 | 00:24 - 00:36
It's so much more complex. Complex. If you think you're just gonna come in and do this business and knock away these very big players, I'd say you should really understand what the business is before you decide to commit your capital.

Speaker 2 | 00:43 - 01:16
Today on our priors, we're talking with Glenn Fogel, the CEO of Booking Holdings, a twenty something year veteran of the company who joined when it was just Priceline and worth a few $100,000,000 in the year 2000 and has since helped grow it to a $100,000,000,000 plus company with multiple massive assets across travel that are now being used in the AI era. We will talk about a variety of topics with Glenn that span everything from, the growth and changes that have happened in bookings over time, as well as his own career and perspective on AI. So, Glenn, thank you so much for joining us today.

Speaker 1 | 01:16 - 01:18
Well, thank you very much for having me.

Speaker 2 | 01:18 - 01:37
Yeah. So you've had a really interesting career. You know, you went to Wharton, you went to Harvard Law. Along the way, you worked in, I think, the MIS program at Morgan Stanley. I'd love to just hear a little bit about your early background and how that led you eventually to booking.com and eventually Booking Holdings.

Speaker 1 | 01:37 - 02:00
Sure. I can do that. So real short, I came out of work with Green Finance, and now I end up at the back office in MIS. My first job is putting tapes on a drive in a data center of IBM thirty eighty fours and the discs that went and and the tape drives and put the put the tapes on on the drive. That's where I started.

Speaker 1 | 02:00 - 02:15
There's an operator. Actually, being an operator of a mainframe, very different. Nothing that you prepared for that in college for that. And I then become you become a developer, and you but I basically learned that this is not a career for me. I should do something else.

Speaker 1 | 02:15 - 02:41
And all the people I knew, undergrad and were and were going off to investment bank and made all this money, and I thought, fuck. Try to do that, but you can't go. I've now gone down one shoot, and you can't just jump out of that one to go become an investment banker. So I said, oh, I I agree to finance warrant, Harvard let me into law school, so I'll do that because that's another route. I did that, and I end up getting a job on Wall Street as a banker.

Speaker 1 | 02:41 - 03:04
I did that for till '95 till 1995. And then the bank was bought by another bank, and they fired all the bankers almost, not every bank. Fired most, including me, but not everybody because they fired everybody because they well, they fired everybody. But they didn't fire everybody. So you there was a there was actually some people were picked to stay.

Speaker 1 | 03:05 - 03:31
That was not one of them. That that's pretty bad. So a real good lesson, though, having been fired and knowing what it's like, that is something that I've kept with me throughout my career about how to do it right and how to do it wrong and understand what goes through the other person when you tell them that I'm sorry, but there's no longer a room for you here and this is not the place for you to be. And I I really learned that firsthand being on that side of the table. And so now I'm unemployed.

Speaker 1 | 03:31 - 03:50
And my father had just died, not that long before this happened. My father's died, lost a job. My grandmother died, and he and the dog died, which was kind of sad. And I'm like, what do want to do with my life now? My young thirties, I'm alone, I'm saying no, whatever.

Speaker 1 | 03:50 - 04:03
And I said, you know, I always wanted to write a book. So I start writing a book, I write a novel, I get it done, and now I'm going try and get it published. And it's not really self publishing, man. You weren't doing it on your own. You're trying to get some agent to pick it up.

Speaker 1 | 04:03 - 04:19
And I was introduced to a woman as a a blind date girlfriend. And I think she was a lawyer, but I'm sorry. I don't know. They said, well, she used to work at Random House as an editor. I said, oh, I'm very interested in how you did the And so we have a date, and the book never gets published.

Speaker 1 | 04:19 - 04:35
But I do end up marrying her and have two great kids and has wonderful life. But while I was trying to get the book, you know, an agent to be interested in it, eventually, she said, you know, if this relationship's gonna go forward, you should get a job. I said, damn. Boy, I should. I didn't wanna go back to banking.

Speaker 1 | 04:35 - 04:46
And I said, I don't know. And a friend of mine from law school was a senior type person at Morgan Stanley. And he I told him, look. Amy says I have to get a job. How do thought?

Speaker 1 | 31:59 - 32:09
And, yeah, we're not curing cancer. I know that. But I think that travel is a very important thing for a lot of people. It really adds to their their lives. You know, we can do it better.

Speaker 1 | 32:09 - 32:26
Look. Our mission is to make it easier for everybody to experience the world, and I believe that does improve everybody. It improves the world by getting people to travel more, experience other cultures, other people, etcetera. And we can do it right and make it easier. That's great.

Speaker 1 | 32:26 - 32:35
I wanna be part. I wanna help do that. I do believe that's adding something. And, that's part of the everybody needs to understand, I believe. Why are you doing what you're doing?

Speaker 1 | 32:35 - 32:44
You only get one life. You get one life now. Sometimes people don't have choices at all. That's the only job they have. They need to afford to be able to support their family.

Speaker 1 | 32:44 - 32:57
I got that. Believe that. I know that. But for people who have a little bit of ability to choose, I'd say choose wisely. Choose wisely because you will not get that time back.

Speaker 1 | 32:58 - 33:38
And some people have different ideas on what will they believe their life should be and that whatever it is, it's long term for going that path. That's great. My biggest fear too much is people who've taken paths that in the end, they're middle aged or later, and they're a little bit wistful about, gee, I wonder if I had done it next. And I'll be perfectly honest and well, hope not to many of my law school classmates, I hope, but I fear that too many of them, they chose going to law school because that was just kind of like a path. Then they became lawyers just because, you know, that's normally what you do when you come out of law school, couple of lawyers.

Speaker 1 | 33:38 - 33:57
I didn't, but many people did. And then it paid really well and it was easy in their comfort zone. And then later in life, it was kind of like, gee, what did I do? And that's when I think everybody should really think hard about making sure you choose wisely.

Speaker 2 | 33:57 - 34:08
You know, one thing we've talked about quite a bit is the AI impact on jobs. And, you know, there's this claim jobs apocalypse and all these other things coming. Would love to hear your views on that and how you think about that.

Speaker 1 | 34:08 - 34:29
So it is really interesting. I mean, we take it in terms of the general sense of technology being job replacement. That's something that happened forever. We well, look at the way the agrarian societies went more towards urbanization as technology passed, industrial revolution. We can go through anything like that.

Speaker 1 | 34:29 - 35:00
And so we know that happened. The issue that's really interesting though is the speed of the change. So if you look right now where we are, I've seen it happen over my time just this company. So at the beginning of the company, we were dealing at booking.com or we acquired booking.com. They were doing hotel reservations in over 40 languages, which meant all the content, All the scripts, everything was in 40 languages, and this customer service, 40 languages, all that.

Speaker 1 | 35:00 - 35:19
It's wonderful. And there were a lot of people involved with that because all the translation is now in the oh, it's it's, like, 2005. All the translations are being done by human beings. There's no machine translation at all at the time. And all those customer service human beings, we had to have people in all these 40 different languages and that.

Speaker 1 | 35:19 - 35:28
That was a big deal. Now now we have machine translations. All those jobs are gone. There's nobody doing anything. That's those all those jobs disappear.

Speaker 1 | 35:28 - 35:35
So what happened to those people? Where did they go? What are the tops are they now taking? What are they doing in sec? So that's an example where we see that happen in real time.

Speaker 1 | 35:35 - 36:11
And now we have the issue not only of Apple, now the fear of, well, will I get a job coming out of university because all the jobs seem to be gone because what was necessary was an analyst at a financial department, a bank, or a corporation's job was not being done through basically AI. So the job started, what's going on, how's it going affect? Now we know also the other side, that new jobs aren't gonna be created. We all know that also by history. We're looking at the problem of this, the speed of job, well, disappearance and new job creation.

Speaker 1 | 36:11 - 36:41
Those rates are not happening probably the same rate. And the second thing is, what about the people who are not able to make that change? So I think well, I always think about the typical person I think about is a 50 year old truck driver. And when the person who was making a very nice living and felt very good about himself because he or she is driving an 18 wheeler across The US and very responsible, helping contribute to a good job there. And now all of sudden, that's completely automated.

Speaker 1 | 36:42 - 36:49
Mhmm. You're out of a job. What are we gonna how are we gonna retrain that pro? What's that person? That person's gonna feel really bad.

Speaker 1 | 36:49 - 37:18
And we've seen in society how these type of forced dislocations have caused problems in the past when you look at that. And I'm concerned that there's not enough thought being done about how are we going to deal with these changes if they happen too quickly or not. Society is whole, absolutely. It's always benefit from technology and creating and being able to do more things. But we have to how are we gonna deal with the flip side, the cost that come with it.

Speaker 2 | 37:18 - 37:22
Do have a do have a specific viewpoint or proposal in terms of what we should be doing there?

Speaker 1 | 37:22 - 37:42
Well, I'll tell you one thing that we do here at our company. One thing that we're always doing is trying to upskill people. I'd say every day, I'm talking with my head of the my CHR overhead of, you know, how can we do the best training? How can we get people so they are ready for the future? How do we get them so they are becoming AI literate?

Speaker 1 | 37:42 - 38:01
It's quite a word that these AI be able to do AI. And that's really important because even if we end up that we can't replace or retrain or put someone else, at least they are better skilled for a job somewhere else. And I feel a real obligation for that. Now that's that's our point. I think everybody should be thinking that way too.

Speaker 1 | 38:01 - 38:09
It's good for our company. It's a positive for our life for somebody to be able to use new tools in a better way. They're more productive. That's great. It's good.

Speaker 1 | 38:09 - 38:32
But it also helps them for their career. Now, sometimes I could see somebody in short term saying, I don't wanna spend the money for that. And it's not the right way to think about it. Now, we could have governments coming in with certain types of programs trying to come up with ways, but retraining by governments over the last fifty years really hasn't worked out so well. So I'm not sure that's the right way to go either.

Speaker 1 | 38:32 - 39:15
But I do believe this is something that I really would like to have more conversation about how to do it because I do I am concerned if we end up in a situation where people will start rejecting technology because of fear, that would end up being bad for us as a society, and by the way, other parts of world are not gonna have that problem, and they will be disadvantaged, I assure you, in China. They not having that same thing about, Oh, AI is bad, and we shouldn't do it. That is not what's happening there. So I really think we got to talk honesty and openly so we have the right conversation and do not end up on the bad side of people coming out and rejecting what actually are going to be good for society.

Speaker 2 | 39:15 - 39:28
That makes sense. Yeah. It's interesting too because I know that, I know at least one group that has rerun consumer surveys on AI because there's this claim right now that people are very negative on AI. And it turns out it depends on what the question you ask is.

Speaker 1 | 39:28 - 39:29
If you

ask people, do you love using ChatGPT and Gemini and Claude and all that, they're like, we love it, we'll pay more for it, and it's wonderful, helping our lives in all these different ways or it's helping our kids with school or whatever it is. Then if you ask them, are you worried that AI will come and destroy your life and take your job and ruin your career? Then, of course, people are like, I don't like it. And so I think the the questions are being asked a certain way on purpose. And I think to your point, we need to be level headed and say, okay.

Speaker 2 | 39:55 - 40:09
What's the real implication for different areas of the economy? How do we make sure that people benefit overall? And how do we make sure that people can participate? And that's very different from, you know, taking a pure doomer view or taking a pure sort of negative view on what's coming. So, you know, I appreciate your perspective on that.

Speaker 1 | 40:09 - 40:30
You're so right. Part of the problem is in a democracy, which we have, which I'm in favor of, you'll have people who are saying certain things because they're not missing what they believe, but people will give them a vote. And that's also problematic in any or in favor of democracy, but I'll be in favor of people to be a little bit more honest on what they're saying.

Speaker 2 | 40:30 - 40:31
Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. What's their real purpose? Yeah.

Speaker 2 | 40:31 - 40:39
100%. Yeah. Thank you so much for joining today. Really appreciate, you know, you sharing your various views across all these topics. It's been really great chatting with you.

Speaker 1 | 40:39 - 40:44
Well, thank you and congratulations on all things that you've accomplished. It's pretty impressive.
